fuzzy_prd: We need a reusable data transformation and API output library that converts internal application objects into clean, client-facing API responses. The library should let developers define how individual records map to their public representation — dropping any internal or private fields, normalizing certain field types, and declaring which related entities can optionally be embedded. Callers should be able to request nested relationships using a compact dot-separated syntax in a single string or as a list, and the library should handle de-duplication and implicit parent path expansion automatically. There should be a configurable safeguard against runaway nesting depth, with a default cap that should match the way the include parsing module works today. Relationship directives can carry named modifier parameters using a special colon-and-parentheses syntax, with pipe-delimited values; these parameters must be exposed via a read-only container that behaves like the immutable bag used in the auth module, throwing errors on any mutation attempt. The final output format should be pluggable: one mode wraps everything under a top-level key, another outputs fields directly at the root level for compact responses, and a third side-loads related entities into a separate section to avoid duplication across records. All three modes should support attaching arbitrary metadata and pagination information — either page-based with previous/next link generation or cursor-based. A single runnable adapter should translate JSON commands into library calls and print results to stdout, normalizing any internal errors into a neutral format.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: public function createData(ResourceInterface $resource, $scopeIdentifier = null, Scope $parentScopeInstance = null)

trigger_keywords: how many include segments allowed by default
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default recursion limit is 10. Include paths are trimmed to at most 10 dot-separated segments before ancestor expansion. Segments beyond position 10 are silently discarded.

Modifier parameter values within a single modifier are pipe-delimited (the '|' character). Multiple modifiers on the same include are separated by colons after the include name, e.g. foo:limit(5|1):order(-something).

trigger_keywords: error when includes is null or number
what happens if includes is not string or array
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the includes argument is neither a string nor an array (e.g. null or an integer), the system outputs exactly 'error=invalid_includes_argument\n' and produces no normalized list.

trigger_keywords: what happens on param bag write attempt
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any attempt to set or unset a key on the parameter bag throws a LogicException with message 'Modifying parameters is not permitted'. The adapter catches this and outputs exactly 'error=immutable_parameters\n'.

trigger_keywords: missing param bag key return value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Fetching a key that does not exist from the parameter bag returns null. The has/offsetExists check returns false for absent keys.

trigger_keywords: how is a single item wrapped in data mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With the data-wrapped serializer, a single item is emitted as {"data": {...}} and a collection as {"data": [...]}. Both item and collection always use the 'data' key regardless of any resource key supplied.

trigger_keywords: how does array serializer handle a single item
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With the array (flat) serializer, a single item's fields appear directly at the top level with no wrapper key. A collection is placed under the supplied resource key, or under 'data' if no resource key is given.

trigger_keywords: json api serializer single item format
how does json_api serializer wrap a single record
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: With the JSON API (compound document) serializer, even a single item is wrapped inside an array and placed under the supplied resource key (or 'data' if none given), e.g. {"book": [{...}]}. Included relationships are collected into a top-level 'linked' section, not inline.

A 'previous' link is only present when current_page > 1. A 'next' link is only present when current_page < last_page. Each link is base_url + '?page=' + adjacent_page_number. The pagination block uses 'total_pages' (not 'last_page') to report the last page value.

trigger_keywords: what fields appear in cursor block under meta
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cursor block under 'meta' contains exactly four fields: 'current', 'prev', 'next', and 'count' (count is cast to int). The block key is 'cursor' nested inside 'meta', e.g. {"meta": {"cursor": {"current": 0, "prev": "ban", "next": "ban", "count": 2}}}.

trigger_keywords: which fields are dropped during transformation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During transformation, any field whose name begins with an underscore ('_') is dropped from the output. For example, '_author' is removed from the visible record. Additionally, the 'year' field is normalized to an integer if present.

A relationship declared as a default include is always emitted even when the caller does not explicitly request it. Default includes are set via setDefaultIncludes() on the transformer. With the array serializer, the relationship data is merged inline at the top level; with the data serializer, it is nested under a 'data' key beside the parent fields.

trigger_keywords: how are duplicate related entities handled in compound document mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In the JSON API serializer's 'linked' section, related entities are deduplicated by their 'id' field. If the same id appears more than once across the primary records, only the first occurrence is kept. Entities without an 'id' field are always included without deduplication.

trigger_keywords: how does requesting a nested include affect parent paths
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a dotted nested path such as 'bar.baz' is requested, all ancestor paths are automatically added. So 'bar.baz' causes both 'bar' and 'bar.baz' to appear in the requested includes list. This expansion happens after the recursion limit trim.

trigger_keywords: what happens with duplicate include entries
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Duplicate include entries are collapsed to a single occurrence. The order of first appearance is preserved. For example, ['foo', 'bar', 'bar'] results in ['foo', 'bar'].

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the immutable bag used in the auth module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ParamBag class in golden/src/ParamBag.php — an ArrayAccess implementation that throws LogicException on any offsetSet or offsetUnset call, used to expose read-only modifier parameters for include directives.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the way the include parsing module works today
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Manager::parseIncludes() method in golden/src/Manager.php, which enforces the default recursion limit of 10 via Manager::trimToAcceptableRecursionLevel() and the $recursionLimit property defaulting to 10.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That aspect is not directly specified in the public test contracts. Please refer to the source code in the golden/ directory, particularly Manager.php, Scope.php, and the Serializer classes, for authoritative implementation details.

feature1_1_parse_includes.json.description: Normalize a set of requested relationship-include directives. The directives may be supplied as a comma-separated string or as a list of strings. The result is the normalized, de-duplicated list of requested include paths: repeated entries collapse to one, and any dotted (nested) path implicitly adds each of its ancestor paths so a request for a child also requests every parent leading to it. Order of first appearance is preserved.

feature1_2_recursion_limit.json.description: Limit how deeply nested include paths may go. Each dotted include path is trimmed to at most a configurable number of segments (default ten); any segments beyond the limit are discarded before the ancestor-expansion happens. The result is the normalized list of include paths after trimming.

feature1_3_include_params.json.description: Parse modifier parameters attached to an include directive. A directive may carry one or more named modifiers using the syntax name(values) appended after a colon, and each modifier's values are pipe-delimited. After parsing, the parameters for a given include can be queried by modifier name, returning the list of that modifier's values; querying a modifier that was not supplied returns null.

feature1_4_invalid_includes.json.description: Reject an include argument that is neither a string nor a list. When the include argument has an unsupported type, parsing fails and a neutral error category is reported instead of any output.

feature2_param_bag.json.description: Provide read-only access to a bag of parameters. Stored values (scalars or lists) can be fetched by key; fetching an absent key yields null and an existence check reports whether a key is present. The bag is immutable: any attempt to assign or remove a parameter is rejected with a neutral error category rather than mutating the bag.

feature3_data_serializer.json.description: Serialize transformed resources under a fixed top-level data key. A single record is wrapped as an object under data; a list of records is wrapped as an array under data. Each record is transformed first (the year field is normalized to an integer and private underscore-prefixed fields are dropped). When a nested relationship is requested, the related resource is itself wrapped under its own data key and placed beside the parent's fields. Any attached metadata is emitted under a separate top-level meta key.

feature4_array_serializer.json.description: Serialize transformed resources without a data wrapper. A single record's fields appear directly at the top level. A list of records appears as an array under the supplied resource key, or under a default data key when no resource key is given. Nested included relationships are merged in flat, keyed by the relationship name and not wrapped. Records are transformed first (year normalized to integer, private underscore-prefixed fields dropped). A relationship marked as a default include is emitted even when not explicitly requested. Attached metadata is emitted under a separate top-level meta key.

feature5_jsonapi_serializer.json.description: Serialize transformed resources as a compound document with side-loaded relationships. The primary record(s) are placed as an array under the supplied resource key (or a default data key when none is given); a single record still appears inside an array. Included relationships are not nested inline but collected into a separate top-level linked section, grouped by relationship name. When the same related entity (identified by its id) appears more than once across the primary records, it is listed only once in the linked section. Records are transformed first (year normalized to integer, private underscore-prefixed fields dropped). Attached metadata is emitted under a separate top-level meta key.

feature6_1_paginator_meta.json.description: Attach page-based pagination metadata to a serialized collection. Given the current page, last page, per-page size, total item count, and count on the current page, the serialized output carries a pagination block under meta containing those figures (with total_pages reflecting the last page) plus a links section. A previous link is present only when the current page is past the first page, and a next link is present only when the current page precedes the last page; each link is the page URL for the adjacent page.

feature6_2_cursor_meta.json.description: Attach cursor pagination metadata to a serialized collection. Given the current cursor position, the previous and next cursor values, and the number of items in the current window, the serialized output carries a cursor block under meta reporting those four values.
